title: Hammer a Nail
summary: Hold a nail steady, aim the hammer, and drive a nail straight into wood without bending it or hitting your fingers.
learnerPromise: Hammer a nail straight without bending it or hitting your fingers.
whyItMatters: Hanging a picture, building a simple shelf, or fixing a loose board all start with driving a nail. It is a basic home skill that opens the door to many small projects.
realLifeUses: Hang a picture frame or poster on a wall
Secure a loose piece of wood trim
Build a simple hook or bracket
youWillLearn: How to hold a hammer by the end of the handle for better control
How to steady a nail with your fingers safely
How to tap lightly first to set the nail, then drive it in
How to aim so the nail goes in straight
miniChallenge: Hammer one nail into a piece of wood, keeping it straight.
steps: Place the piece of wood on a firm, stable surface that can handle a few taps.
Hold the nail near the pointed end, not the head, and position the tip where you want it.
Grip the hammer near the end of the handle and give the nail head one or two light taps to set it in place.
Move your fingers away from the nail and drive it in with firm, straight strikes.
Check that the nail is going in straight, and stop when the head is flush with the wood surface.
completionCriteria: The nail is mostly straight and securely in the wood
The nail head sits flush with or just above the surface
commonProblems: Nail bent to the side — it may have hit a knot or been struck at an angle; pull it out and try a new nail nearby
Hit fingers instead of the nail — use a clothespin or pliers to hold the nail for the first few taps
Nail goes in crooked — pause after each tap to check alignment and adjust your angle
tips: Start with a larger nail; they are easier to hit than small finishing nails
Use the weight of the hammer to do most of the work rather than swinging too hard
Practice on a scrap piece of wood first to build confidence
